summary: This PR introduces a new `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` to normalize the exception raised by `response.json()` across Python versions and JSON backends. While the intent is sound, there are several correctness gaps — most critically, a `JSONDecodeError` from the `guess_json_utf` early-return path escapes unwrapped — plus meaningful test coverage gaps around inheritance guarantees, the Python 2 branch, and the simplejson code path. Style and documentation nits are minor.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError from guess_json_utf early-return path propagates unwrapped
findings.detail: When `guess_json_utf` successfully detects an encoding, `complexjson.loads(self.content.decode(encoding), **kwargs)` is called (lines 899–901). If the decoded content is not valid JSON, a raw `json.JSONDecodeError` (or `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`) is raised here, but only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught in this block (line 902). The raw library-level `JSONDecodeError` propagates directly to the caller instead of being re-raised as `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError`. This directly breaks the PR's stated invariant that `response.json()` always raises `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` on failure, and creates the same inconsistency the PR is trying to fix.

findings.title: No tests verify JSONDecodeError inheritance/aliasing guarantees
findings.detail: A primary goal of this PR is backwards compatibility: `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` should be catchable as `json.JSONDecodeError`, `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`, `ValueError`, and `requests.exceptions.RequestException`. The new test only checks that `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` is raised; it never asserts that the exception is also an instance of `ValueError`, `json.JSONDecodeError`, or `requests.exceptions.RequestException`. Additionally, there are no isolated unit tests that construct `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` directly, verify its MRO, or confirm `isinstance` checks against `InvalidJSONError`, `RequestException`, and `CompatJSONDecodeError`. These aliasing/inheritance guarantees are completely untested.

findings.title: requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError does not subclass both json.JSONDecodeError and simplejson.JSONDecodeError; simplejson branch untested
findings.detail: The PR description states the new error 'aliases both' standard-library and simplejson `JSONDecodeError`, but the implementation selects only one at import time based on `has_simplejson`. When simplejson is installed, `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` inherits from `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` but NOT from `json.JSONDecodeError`, so user code that catches `json.JSONDecodeError` will NOT catch `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` in a simplejson environment. This silently breaks the backwards-compatibility guarantee. Furthermore, there is no test (in `test_packages.py` or elsewhere) that verifies the correct alias is selected when simplejson is available, leaving the `has_simplejson=True` branch entirely untested.

findings.title: Python 2 branch untested and uses deprecated `.message` attribute
findings.detail: The `if is_py2:` branch at line 914 raises `RequestsJSONDecodeError(e.message)`. First, `.message` is a deprecated attribute removed from `BaseException` in Python 2.6+ — accessing it returns an empty string rather than the actual error message; `str(e)` or `e.args[0]` should be used instead. Second, if a third-party JSON library raises a `ValueError` subclass without `.message`, an `AttributeError` will be raised instead of the intended `RequestsJSONDecodeError`. Third, this entire branch has no test coverage whatsoever — the only new test always exercises the `else` path — so the `e.message` access is entirely untested.

findings.title: Test coverage gaps: only one trigger path tested; None-content and invalid-JSON-text paths missing
findings.detail: The new `test_json_decode_compatibility` test only covers the `/bytes/20` random-bytes path, which may fail at charset detection rather than JSON parsing. It does not test: (1) a well-formed HTTP response whose body is syntactically invalid JSON (e.g. a plaintext `'not json'` string), which exercises the separate code path at lines 909–917 rather than 891–907; and (2) the behavior when `self.text` is `None` — the existing test for `test_response_json_when_content_is_None` may still expect a plain `ValueError` rather than `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError`, and if `complexjson.loads(None)` raises a `TypeError` it will propagate uncaught through the new `except` block.

findings.title: Potential MRO conflict when CompatJSONDecodeError is ValueError on Python 2
findings.detail: On Python 2, `CompatJSONDecodeError` is `ValueError`. `InvalidJSONError` inherits from `RequestException` → `IOError`. The combined `JSONDecodeError(InvalidJSONError, ValueError)` MRO places `IOError` before `ValueError`, and on CPython 2 certain C-extension base combinations can raise `TypeError: Cannot create a consistent method resolution order` or a `TypeError` at instantiation. Confidence is moderate as Python 2 behavior varies across patch versions and environments.

findings.title: Exception chaining suppressed — original exception context lost
findings.detail: The `raise RequestsJSONDecodeError(...)` statements do not use `raise ... from e`, so the original exception cause is not chained. This discards `__cause__`/`__context__`, making it harder for logging frameworks and debuggers to trace the root cause. Using `raise RequestsJSONDecodeError(...) from e` would preserve the chain.

findings.title: Import-time simplejson inconsistency possible in mocked test environments
findings.detail: The `has_simplejson` flag and `from simplejson import JSONDecodeError` are set independently at module-import time. If a test or mocking framework replaces `simplejson` in `sys.modules` between these two points, `CompatJSONDecodeError` could be inconsistent with the `complexjson` module actually used for parsing, causing the `except JSONDecodeError` block in `models.py` to miss the raised error and let a raw exception escape. This is a low-severity defense-in-depth concern primarily relevant in test environments.

findings.title: Docstring style inconsistency: lowercase 'json' and missing trailing period
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file use a capitalised, period-terminated docstring (e.g. "An HTTP error occurred."). The new class uses "Couldn't decode the text into json" — lowercase 'j' in 'json' and no trailing period. Should be "Couldn't decode the text into JSON." to match the convention.

findings.title: "python" should be capitalised to "Python" in documentation
findings.detail: The surrounding documentation consistently uses the capitalised form "Python". Line 157 reads "different python versions" — "python" should be "Python".

findings.title: Missing newline at end of file
findings.detail: The file ends without a trailing newline. All other source files in the project include a trailing newline, and the prior version of this file did as well. A newline should be added.

findings.title: New import should be merged into the existing grouped import block
findings.detail: Every other internal import in models.py is grouped into a single `from .exceptions import (...)` block (lines 30–33). The new `from .exceptions import JSONDecodeError as RequestsJSONDecodeError` on its own separate line is inconsistent with that style and should be added to the existing import tuple.

findings.title: Missing second space before inline comment (PEP 8)
findings.detail: PEP 8 and the surrounding code style require two spaces before an inline comment. `if is_py2: # e is a ValueError` has only one space before `#`. The rest of the file's inline comments use two spaces.

findings.title: Blank line removed between import block and surrounding code
findings.detail: The original code had a blank line separating the try/except json-import block from the surrounding code. The diff removes that blank line (between `has_simplejson = True` and `except ImportError`). The surrounding style consistently uses blank lines to separate logical groups.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError from early-return path not caught or re-raised as RequestsJSONDecodeError
findings.detail: When `guess_json_utf` successfully detects an encoding, `complexjson.loads(self.content.decode(encoding), **kwargs)` is called (line 899-901). If that call raises a `JSONDecodeError` (e.g. the content decoded successfully but is not valid JSON), the exception is NOT caught here — only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught (line 902). The raw `json.JSONDecodeError` or `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` propagates to the caller instead of the new `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError`. This is inconsistent with the fix's goal and breaks the invariant that `response.json()` always raises `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` on failure.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError MRO conflict when CompatJSONDecodeError is ValueError (Python 2)
findings.detail: On Python 2, `CompatJSONDecodeError` is `ValueError`. `InvalidJSONError` inherits from `RequestException` which inherits from `IOError`. `JSONDecodeError(InvalidJSONError, ValueError)` places `InvalidJSONError`/`IOError` before `ValueError` in the MRO. Python's `__init__` of `ValueError` expects positional args `(msg,)`, while `RequestException.__init__` uses `*args, **kwargs` and pops `response`/`request` kwargs. When `raise RequestsJSONDecodeError(e.message)` is called in models.py line 915, only a positional arg is passed, so `RequestException.__init__` will call `super().__init__(e.message)` which eventually reaches `ValueError.__init__`. This should work for construction, but the MRO resolution of `super()` calls could be fragile if `IOError` and `ValueError` have conflicting C-level layouts — on CPython 2 this raises `TypeError: Error when calling the metaclass bases / Cannot create a consistent method resolution order` or a `TypeError` at instantiation for certain C-extension base combinations. Confidence is moderate because Python 2 behavior here can vary.

findings.title: ValueError from Python 2 json may not have a `.message` attribute
findings.detail: On Python 2, `json.JSONDecodeError` does not exist; a plain `ValueError` is raised. `ValueError` instances in Python 2 do have a `.message` attribute (deprecated but present), however if a future-compat shim or a third-party JSON library raises a `ValueError` subclass without `.message`, this will raise an `AttributeError` instead of the intended `RequestsJSONDecodeError`. A safer approach is to use `str(e)` or `e.args[0]` which works for all `ValueError` subclasses.

findings.title: simplejson.JSONDecodeError imported as CompatJSONDecodeError but simplejson may not actually be used for json.loads
findings.detail: In the py3 + simplejson branch, `JSONDecodeError` is imported from `simplejson` (line 72). However, `import simplejson as json` at line 33 means `complexjson` is `simplejson`, so `complexjson.loads` will raise `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` — this is consistent. BUT if `has_simplejson` is True and simplejson is imported, then `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` inherits from `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`. If `has_simplejson` is False, it inherits from `json.JSONDecodeError`. This means the class hierarchy is determined at import time depending on the environment, which is the intended design. However, a subtle issue: `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` will NOT be a subclass of `json.JSONDecodeError` when simplejson is installed, so user code catching `json.JSONDecodeError` will NOT catch `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` in the simplejson case. The PR description says the new error 'aliases both', but the implementation only aliases one or the other — not both simultaneously.

findings.title: Use of deprecated `.message` attribute on Python 2 exception
findings.detail: On Python 2, `e.message` is a deprecated attribute that was removed from `BaseException` in Python 2.6+; accessing it silently returns an empty string in most cases rather than the actual error message. The actual string value of a Python 2 `ValueError` should be obtained with `str(e)` or `e.args[0]`. This means `RequestsJSONDecodeError` raised on Python 2 may carry an empty or misleading message, potentially masking useful diagnostic information. While the Python 2 path is lower risk from a security perspective, it can hide injection-related error details if the error itself surfaces as a user-facing response.

findings.title: simplejson imported at class-definition time — import confusion if simplejson is swapped after import
findings.detail: The `has_simplejson` flag is set at module-import time (line 31), but `from simplejson import JSONDecodeError` at line 72 re-imports from simplejson independently. If a test or adversarial code replaces `simplejson` in `sys.modules` between these two points (unlikely in normal use, but possible in test environments with module mocking), the `CompatJSONDecodeError` class used as a base in `exceptions.py` could be inconsistent with the `complexjson` module actually used for parsing, meaning the `except JSONDecodeError` block in `models.py` (line 911) would not catch the error thrown by the parser. This is a low-severity defense-in-depth concern — the net effect would be an unhandled exception escaping instead of a `RequestsJSONDecodeError`, but it could expose raw internal tracebacks to callers.

findings.title: Exception chaining suppressed — original exception context lost
findings.detail: The `raise RequestsJSONDecodeError(...)` statements (lines 915 and 917) do not use `raise ... from e`, so the original exception cause is not chained. While this is primarily a correctness/debugging concern, from a security standpoint it means that any introspection or logging framework that reads `__cause__` / `__context__` on the raised exception to detect or report injection-related decode failures (e.g., log-injection attempts embedded in malformed JSON) will silently miss the original exception detail. Consider `raise RequestsJSONDecodeError(...) from e` to preserve the chain.

findings.title: Docstring doesn't follow surrounding style
findings.detail: All other exception classes in this file use a capitalised, period-terminated docstring (e.g. "A JSON error occurred.", "An HTTP error occurred."). The new class uses "Couldn't decode the text into json" — lowercase 'j' in 'json' and no trailing period. Should be "Couldn't decode the text into json." or better "Couldn't decode the text into JSON." to match the convention.

findings.title: Separate import line breaks the single grouped-import convention used in the rest of the file
findings.detail: Every other internal import in models.py is grouped into a single `from .exceptions import (...)` block. The new `from .exceptions import JSONDecodeError as RequestsJSONDecodeError` on its own line is inconsistent with that style. It should be added to the existing import tuple on lines 30-33.

findings.title: Missing space before inline comment
findings.detail: PEP 8 / surrounding code style requires two spaces before an inline comment. `if is_py2: # e is a ValueError` has only one space before `#`. The rest of the file's inline comments use two spaces.

findings.title: Blank line removed between `import simplejson` block and following code
findings.detail: The original code had a blank line separating the try/except json-import block from the surrounding code. The diff removes that blank line (between `has_simplejson = True` and `except ImportError`). While minor, the surrounding style consistently uses blank lines to separate logical groups.

findings.title: "python" should be capitalised to "Python" in documentation
findings.detail: The surrounding documentation consistently uses the capitalised form "Python" (e.g. "on Python 2", "on Python 3"). Line 157 reads "different python versions" — "python" should be "Python".

findings.title: Missing newline at end of file
findings.detail: The file ends without a trailing newline (`\ No newline at end of file`). The existing codebase convention (and the prior version of this same file) expects a newline at the end. This is consistent across all other source files in the project.

findings.title: Python 2 branch of json() error handling has no test coverage
findings.detail: The `if is_py2:` branch at line 914 raises `RequestsJSONDecodeError(e.message)`. The only new test (`test_json_decode_compatibility`) always runs through the `else` path (`e.msg, e.doc, e.pos`). There is no test that exercises the Python 2 code path, meaning the `e.message` attribute access is entirely untested.

findings.title: No test verifies the raised JSONDecodeError inherits from RequestException / can be caught as ValueError
findings.detail: A primary goal of this PR is backwards compatibility: `requests.JSONDecodeError` should be catchable as `json.JSONDecodeError`, `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`, `ValueError`, and `requests.RequestException`. The new test only checks that `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` is raised; it does not assert that the exception can also be caught via `ValueError`, `json.JSONDecodeError`, or `requests.exceptions.RequestException`. These aliasing/inheritance guarantees are completely untested.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError class itself has no unit-level tests
findings.detail: The new `JSONDecodeError` exception class in `exceptions.py` is only exercised through an integration test that hits a live httpbin server. There are no isolated unit tests that construct `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` directly, verify its MRO, confirm it passes `isinstance` checks against `InvalidJSONError`, `RequestException`, `CompatJSONDecodeError`, or test its message/doc/pos attributes after construction.

findings.title: simplejson-installed code path for JSONDecodeError in compat.py is not tested
findings.detail: Lines 71-72 select `simplejson.JSONDecodeError` when `has_simplejson` is True. There is no test (in `test_packages.py` or elsewhere) that verifies the correct `JSONDecodeError` alias is selected when `simplejson` is available, meaning the `has_simplejson=True` branch of this logic is untested.

findings.title: test_json_decode_compatibility only covers one trigger (random bytes response), not invalid JSON text
findings.detail: The test fetches random bytes from `/bytes/20`, which may or may not produce a valid UTF-8 string before hitting `json.loads`. It does not explicitly test the case of a well-formed HTTP response whose body is syntactically invalid JSON (e.g. a plaintext `'not json'` body). The error path triggered by a charset-detection failure vs. a pure JSON parse failure is different code flow (lines 891-907 vs 909-917), and only the latter is exercised by the new test.

findings.title: No test for response.json() when self.text is None (content is None path)
findings.detail: The existing `test_response_json_when_content_is_None` test still expects a plain `ValueError` (line in the old test suite), not `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError`. After this change, if `complexjson.loads(None)` raises a `TypeError` rather than a `JSONDecodeError`, it will propagate uncaught through the new try/except block. There is no test asserting the behaviour when `self.text` is `None` under the new exception handling.

findings.title: JSONDecodeError from guess_json_utf early-return path propagates unwrapped
findings.detail: When `guess_json_utf` successfully detects an encoding, `complexjson.loads(self.content.decode(encoding), **kwargs)` is called (lines 899–901). If the decoded content is not valid JSON, a raw `json.JSONDecodeError` (or `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`) is raised here, but only `UnicodeDecodeError` is caught in this block (line 902). The raw library-level `JSONDecodeError` propagates directly to the caller instead of being re-raised as `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError`. This directly breaks the PR's stated invariant that `response.json()` always raises `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` on failure, and creates the same inconsistency the PR is trying to fix.

findings.detail: A primary goal of this PR is backwards compatibility: `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` should be catchable as `json.JSONDecodeError`, `simplejson.JSONDecodeError`, `ValueError`, and `requests.exceptions.RequestException`. The new test only checks that `requests.exceptions.JSONDecodeError` is raised; it never asserts that the exception is also an instance of `ValueError`, `json.JSONDecodeError`, or `requests.exceptions.RequestException`.
